title: Page-level content with tabs as an optional resource
description: Today all editable content lives inside tabs: `blocks.tab_id` is a NOT NULL FK to `tabs`, and a page with zero tabs has nowhere to hold content. A page should be able to have its own body content directly, with tabs becoming an OPTIONAL way to add extra named views (e.g. 'Usage', 'Code', 'Accessibility'). A brand-new page must be immediately editable without the user having to create a tab first.
acceptanceCriteria: A newly created page is immediately editable and can hold content without the user creating any tab
Tabs are optional: a page with no user-defined tabs shows no tab bar and just renders/edits its body; adding one or more tabs shows them as additional named views alongside (or after) the page body

The content pipeline keeps working end-to-end for page-level content: autosave draft, publish (revision snapshot), history/restore, full-text search indexing, and the public read-only render all include the page body â€” not only tab content
A migration backfills existing pages so no current content is lost (existing tabs and their blocks remain intact and reachable)

The public route and the admin editor route resolve page body content correctly (the current `/pages/:pageId/tabs/:tabId` editor route and `/docs/:sectionSlug/:pageSlug` public route must still work, plus a way to reach the page body itself)

steps.description: Decide and document the data model for page body content
steps.details: Confirm the approach with the project owner. RECOMMENDED (lowest risk): every page owns one implicit 'primary' content container that renders as the page body without tab chrome; user tabs are additional. Implement it by guaranteeing a system primary tab per page (keep `blocks.tab_id`, so serialize/snapshot/publish/restore/FTS in `apps/server/src/db/revisions.ts` + `blocks/serialize.ts` need no structural change), with the migration promoting each existing page's first tab (or creating one) as primary. ALTERNATIVE (larger): make `blocks.tab_id` nullable and add a nullable `blocks.page_id` with a one-of constraint, then rework serialization, `PageSnapshot` (`packages/schema/src/block.ts`), publish/restore, FTS reindex, and public rendering to merge page-level blocks with tab blocks. Pick one before writing code.

Update `apps/server/src/db/schema.ts` per step 1's decision and generate a migration via `pnpm --filter @systembook/server db:generate`. Backfill existing pages so their current content maps to the new page-body model. If the primary-tab approach is chosen, mark the primary container so the UI can distinguish it (e.g. a `tipo`/`is_primary` flag on `tabs`, or a reserved ordem/sentinel) and hide it from the tab bar.

Ensure autosave (`blocks.saveDraft`/`getByTab`), publish snapshot (`buildPageSnapshot`), restore (`restoreRevision`), FTS reindex (`reindexPageFts`), and the `PageSnapshot` shape all account for page-body content. If the primary-tab approach is used, most of this works unchanged but the snapshot/UI must still render the primary container as the page body.

In `apps/admin`: a new page opens straight into an editable body (no 'create a tab first' step). The `SidebarTree` and `TabContentPage` must let the user edit the page body and optionally add tabs; when a page has no user tabs, no tab bar is shown. Keep the publish/history controls at page level (already the case in `TabContentPage.tsx`).

Update the public read-only render (`features/public/PublicPageView.tsx` / `PageRenderer.tsx`) to show the page body, with tabs (if any) as additional views. Verify end-to-end: create a page, write body content without adding a tab, publish, view it on `/docs/...` logged out, then add a tab and confirm both body and tab render. Confirm existing pages still render after the migration.

technicalNotes: This is a cross-cutting change (data model â†’ serialization â†’ publish/restore â†’ FTS â†’ admin editor â†’ public render). Consider splitting into a data-model task + an api task + a ui task if it grows; step 1's decision determines the blast radius.

The primary-tab approach keeps the whole revisions/snapshot/FTS machinery (which is keyed on tabs) intact and is strongly preferred for the MVP; the nullable page_id approach is cleaner conceptually but touches every consumer of blocks.
Watch the FTS reindex and the public sidebar/nav: they currently assume content == tabs. Whatever the model, published page body must be searchable and linkable.
